ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for laddu; A0CKP (sweet confectionery) used as closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for laddu; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: chickpea flour (besan) or semolina or wheat flour

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard laddu ball weighs approximately 40 g and is typically consumed as a single serving during festivals, celebrations, or as a sweet snack.

nutrient_source.title: Laddu — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Laddu (cr_id F11100012). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for besan laddu as the HPB record does not report fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for laddu found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous flour-based Indian sweet confection with comparable sugar (38 g/100g) and refined carbohydrate content (50 g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × available carbohydrate per serving (50.03 g/100g × 40 g serving = 20.01 g) / 100 = 13.0 g. This is an analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (38 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.7 g/100g) triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention; ghee-derived saturated fat contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium (20 mg/100g), laddu is unsuitable for hypertension management due to its very high sugar content (38 g/100g) and low fibre (1.7 g/100g), which drive insulin-mediated sodium retention and vascular inflammation; limit to occasional consumption of no more than half a small laddu.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 38 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling; combined with 50 g carbohydrate/100g and only 1.7 g fibre/100g, laddu causes severe post-meal glucose excursions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Laddu is contraindicated for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its extremely high sugar content (38 g/100g) and high refined carbohydrate load (50 g/100g) with minimal fibre buffering (1.7 g/100g); if consumed on rare festive occasions, limit to half a small laddu (approximately 20 g) and pair with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat from ghee (~10.5 g/100g) raises LDL cholesterol; very high sugar (38 g/100g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Laddu poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its combination of high saturated fat from ghee (~10.5 g/100g) and very high sugar (38 g/100g), which together raise LDL cholesterol, promote hypertriglyceridaemia, and impair endothelial function; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption and limit intake to very small portions on rare occasions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~10.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold, promoting TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; sugar at 38 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Laddu is strongly pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis due to its high ghee-derived saturated fat (~10.5 g/100g) and very high sugar content (38 g/100g), both of which amplify inflammatory cytokine pathways; individuals with RA should avoid laddu entirely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laddu (also spelled laddoo or ladoo) is a traditional Indian sweet confection made by combining flour — most commonly chickpea flour (besan), semolina (rava/sooji), or wheat flour — with sugar or jaggery and ghee (clarified butter), then shaping the mixture into round balls. The sweet is flavoured with cardamom and often garnished with nuts such as cashews, almonds, or pistachios. Laddu has a dense, crumbly, and melt-in-the-mouth texture with an intensely sweet, buttery flavour and a warm aromatic note from cardamom. It is one of the most widely consumed traditional sweets across the Indian subcontinent and is deeply embedded in Hindu religious ceremonies, Diwali celebrations, weddings, and festive gifting. In Singapore and the broader South Asian diaspora, laddu is available at Indian sweet shops and is consumed as a festive treat or dessert. Nutritionally, laddu is energy-dense at 407 kcal/100g, with high fat (20.7 g/100g, predominantly saturated from ghee at approximately 10.5 g/100g), very high sugar (38 g/100g), and moderate protein (5.06 g/100g). Dietary fibre is low at 1.7 g/100g. Sodium is very low at 20 mg/100g. Due to its very high sugar and saturated fat content, laddu is clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis, and should be treated as an occasional indulgence in very small portions.

How much sugar is in laddu?
What is the glycemic index of laddu?

ingredient_synonyms.jaggery: unrefined cane sugar, gur, palm sugar

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact laddu entry (cr_id F11100012). Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for besan laddu. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Overall confidence is rated high given the exact HPB source match for all primary macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at ~10.5 g/100g based on ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 for besan laddu; HPB record does not report fatty acid fractions. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient, and moisture data for laddu are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN tables at the level of this generic record as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for laddu was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 4. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible category anchor for sweet confectionery; no exact FoodEx2 code for laddu exists. 5. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311069 used as closest defensible match; reviewers should verify this URI against the current FoodOn release. 6. Laddu is a generic record covering multiple flour-base variants (besan, rava, wheat); nutrient values reflect the HPB canonical entry and may vary by preparation style.
